content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 1, 1992

Marlene L. Thierbach
Fabozzi, Prenovost & Normandin
2020 East First Street, Ste. 500
Santa Ana, CA 92705

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-92-163

Dear Ms. Thierbach:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act. Because you have not identified the judges
on whose behalf you are requesting this advice we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation
18329(c)(3).²

Your letter accurately summarizes my advice that a judge may
accept a contribution so long as Form 501 is filed prior to
soliciting or receiving the contribution. The contribution must
be deposited into a separate bank account and Form 502 must be
filed within 10 days of the opening of the account. The
contribution may be used to repay loans made from the judge's
personal funds on behalf of his or her election, including
personal funds used to pay the judge's filing fee.

1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sections
18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •.(916) 322-56

Page 2
Thierbach, Marlene


     Section 82013 provides that a committee is formed when a
candidate receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a
calendar year.  However, personal funds which are expended to pay
a filing fee are not counted toward that $1,000.  (Section 84206.)

     If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5662.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Scott Hallabrin
                                   Acting General Counsel
                                   [illegible]
                                   By: Wayne P. Imberi
                                       Political Reform Consultant

sections.conclusion: A judge may accept a contribution so long as Form 501 is filed prior to soliciting or receiving the contribution. The contribution must be deposited into a separate bank account and Form 502 must be filed within 10 days of the opening of the account. The contribution may be used to repay loans made from the judge's personal funds on behalf of his or her election, including personal funds used to pay the judge's filing fee. Personal funds expended to pay a filing fee are not counted toward the $1,000 threshold for committee formation.
sections.question_synthetic: The requestor sought confirmation of telephone advice regarding whether a judge may accept campaign contributions and how such contributions must be handled under California's Political Reform Act, specifically concerning Form 501 and 502 filing requirements and the use of contributions to repay personal loans.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that judges may accept contributions provided Form 501 is filed before solicitation or receipt, contributions are deposited in a separate account with Form 502 filed within 10 days, and contributions may be used to repay personal campaign loans including filing fees. Personal funds used for filing fees do not count toward the $1,000 committee formation threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is confirmation of prior telephone advice formatted as informal written guidance. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers, but substantive legal advice is clearly present. Informal assistance designation means no immunity is provided per Government Code Section 83114.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The requestor sought confirmation of telephone advice regarding whether a judge may accept campaign contributions and how such contributions must be handled under California's Political Reform Act, specifically concerning Form 501 and 502 filing requirements and the use of contributions to repay personal loans.

CONCLUSION: A judge may accept a contribution so long as Form 501 is filed prior to soliciting or receiving the contribution. The contribution must be deposited into a separate bank account and Form 502 must be filed within 10 days of the opening of the account. The contribution may be used to repay loans made from the judge's personal funds on behalf of his or her election, including personal funds used to pay the judge's filing fee. Personal funds expended to pay a filing fee are not counted toward the $1,000 threshold for committee formation.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC informal advice letter confirming telephone advice to an attorney regarding campaign disclosure rules for judges, specifically addressing contribution acceptance, account handling, and use of funds under the Political Reform Act.
